events.label: reasoning
events.text:
The user wants me to assess a trade-work scenario and return exactly one valid JSON object. No prose, no markdown fences.

Scenario: non-IC can in insulation contact. Residential electrical fixture installation. Work stage rough-complete. Reference NEC 410.

Key issue: A "non-IC" (non-insulation contact) recessed luminaire/can is installed in direct contact with thermal insulation. NEC 410.116(A) or thereabouts requires recessed luminaires to be listed and identified for the purpose, and Type IC (insulation contact) rated fixtures are required when installed in insulated ceilings where insulation will be in direct contact. Non-IC fixtures require minimum clearances (typically 3 inches) from insulation and combustibles. So this is a fail. Risk is high because of fire hazard from overheating. Component conditions: installed-defective and non-compliant? The list includes installed-defective, non-compliant, worn, degraded, failed. Non-IC can in insulation contact is non-compliant, and it's installed defective because it's installed in a manner that is defective. The scenario explicitly mentions component condition categories may apply: installed-defective, non-compliant. So include both.

percent_complete: rough-complete, maybe 70-80%? The work is roughed in. Fixture installed. Estimate 75%.

value: not applicable, 0.

sound_source, event_time, rate, order: not audio/video task, leave defaults? The schema includes them. For non-applicable, likely empty string or 0. The example uses 0 for event_time and rate, empty string for sound_source, empty list for order. Keep those.

workable: empty list unless asked. Not asked.

findings: list short defect/hazard findings: "Non-IC rated recessed luminaire installed in direct contact with thermal insulation" and "Fire hazard due to overheating of luminaire" etc.

actions: immediate corrective action: "Replace with IC-rated fixture or relocate/trim insulation to maintain required clearance per manufacturer" etc.

rationale: brief explanation.

references: ["NEC 410.116", "NEC 410.116(A)", "NEC 410.110"] maybe. Need be precise.

NEC 410.116 covers Space for Recessed Luminaires. (A) says Recessed luminaires shall have thermal protection and be identified as suitable for insulation contact unless ... Wait, exact text from memory: 410.116(A) Thermal Insulation. Recessed incandescent luminaires or recessed luminaires with medium base sockets, other than listed airtight Type IC, installed in the ceiling of insulated spaces shall have all recessed portions completely enclosed with noncombustible material... Hmm. Actually, the relevant requirement is that recessed luminaires shall be listed for the purpose and Type IC when in direct contact with insulation. NEC 410.116(A): "Recessed luminaires shall be installed such that the walls of the recessed portions are thermally protected and identified as suitable for insulation contact, or the luminaire shall be installed so that it is not in contact with the insulation and is not within 13 mm (1/2 in.) of the insulation unless ..." Wait, that's for LED? Let me recall accurately.

NEC 410.116 Space for Recessed Luminaires.
(A) Combustible Material. Recessed luminaires shall be installed so that the walls of the recessed portions are thermally protected and identified as suitable for insulation contact, or the luminaire shall be installed so that it is not in contact with the insulation and is not within 13 mm (1/2 in.) of the insulation unless ... Hmm.

Actually, there are two subsections: (A) Thermal Insulation and (B) Combustible Material? Let's check memory.

2020 NEC 410.116 Space for Recessed Luminaires:
(A) Combustible Material. Recessed lumf? Let's search memory.

The exact code: 410.116 Space for Recessed Luminaires.
(A) Combustible Material. Recessed luminaires shall be installed so that adjacent combustible material will not be subjected to temperatures in excess of 90°C (194°F).
(B) Insulation. Recessed luminaires shall be identified for the pur…
